content:
# Mark Chapter 13 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 13 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 13:1 — "wonderful stones" vs. "incredible stones" vs. "What stones!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What stones and what buildings!" | Literal exclamation. |
| LOGOS | "What wonderful stones and what wonderful buildings!" | Adds "wonderful" (ποταποί). |
| PNEUMA | "look at these incredible stones! What magnificent buildings!" | Dynamic; intensifies with "incredible" and "magnificent." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "What wonderful stones and what wonderful buildings!" — ποταπός means "of what kind/how wonderful," and "wonderful" captures the disciples' awe.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:6 — "I am he" vs. "I am the one" for ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am [he]" | Brackets the implied predicate. |
| LOGOS | "'I am he!'" | Standard completion. |
| PNEUMA | "'I am the one!'" | Dynamic; specifies. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "'I am he!'" — consistent with the ΕΓΩ ΕΙΜΙ rendering established throughout Mark.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:8 — "birth pains" vs. "birth-pains" vs. "contractions before the birth"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "beginning of-birth-pains" | Literal; ὠδίνων. |
| LOGOS | "the beginning of the birth pains" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "the first contractions before the birth" | Dynamic; "contractions" specifies. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the beginning of the birth pains" — ὠδίνες is the standard apocalyptic term, and "birth pains" is its established rendering. "Contractions" is a medical term that loses the theological resonance.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 13:9 — "councils" vs. "courts" for συνέδρια

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "They-will-deliver you to councils" | Literal; συνέδρια = councils/courts. |
| LOGOS | "They will deliver you over to councils" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "You'll be dragged before courts" | Dynamic; "dragged" adds force; "courts" modernizes. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "deliver you over to councils" — συνέδρια refers to Jewish local councils (the Sanhedrin system). "Courts" is too modern/generic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 13:14 — "abomination of desolation" vs. "disgusting sacrilege" for τὸ βδέλυγμα τῆς ἐρημώσεως

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the abomination of-desolation" | Traditional; Daniel reference. |
| LOGOS | "the abomination of desolation" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "the disgusting sacrilege" | Dynamic; attempts accessibility. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the abomination of desolation" — this phrase from Daniel 9:27/11:31/12:11 is a technical apocalyptic term with deep intertextual roots. "Disgusting sacrilege" is semantically adequate but loses the biblical resonance that "let the reader understand" depends upon.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 13:14 — "let the reader understand" vs. "reader, pay attention!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "let the-one reading understand" | Literal; ὁ ἀναγινώσκων νοείτω. |
| LOGOS | "let the reader understand" | Formal; standard. |
| PNEUMA | "reader, pay attention!" | Dynamic; direct address. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "let the reader understand" — this is the traditional rendering and correctly conveys the editorial aside. PNEUMA's "pay attention" loses the specific "understand" (νοέω) which implies decoding.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:19 — "tribulation" vs. "suffering" for θλῖψις

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "tribulation such-as has not been" | Traditional; θλῖψις = tribulation/distress. |
| LOGOS | "such tribulation as has not been" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "days of suffering worse than anything" | Dynamic; "suffering" for θλῖψις. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "tribulation" — in apocalyptic discourse, θλῖψις is a technical term (the Great Tribulation), and the traditional rendering preserves this.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 13:20 — "no human being would be saved" vs. "no flesh would-have-been-saved" vs. "no one would survive"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "no flesh would-have-been-saved" | Literal; πᾶσα σάρξ = all flesh. |
| LOGOS | "no human being would be saved" | Formal; interprets σάρξ. |
| PNEUMA | "no one would survive" | Dynamic; drops σάρξ entirely. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "no human being would be saved" — "flesh" as a standalone translation of σάρξ is too opaque; "human being" accurately renders the idiom while "saved" preserves the σῴζω verb.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:22 — "false christs" vs. "fake messiahs" for ψευδόχριστοι

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "false-christs and false-prophets" | Literal; compounds preserved. |
| LOGOS | "false christs and false prophets" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "Fake messiahs and fake prophets" | Dynamic; "fake" for ψευδο-; "messiahs" for Χριστοί. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "false christs and false prophets" — consistent with "Christ" vocabulary established throughout. "Fake" is too casual for the apocalyptic gravity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 13:25 — "the powers in the heavens" vs. "the cosmic powers" for αἱ δυνάμεις αἱ ἐν τοῖς οὐρανοῖς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the powers in the heavens" | Literal. |
| LOGOS | "the powers in the heavens" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "the cosmic powers will be shaken loose" | "Cosmic" modernizes; "shaken loose" adds. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the powers in the heavens" — "cosmic" introduces modern astronomical connotations. The Greek refers to celestial/heavenly powers (angelic or astronomical), and "heavens" preserves the biblical register.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 13:31 — "will pass away" vs. "will disappear" for παρελεύσονται

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "will-pass-away" | Standard rendering. |
| LOGOS | "will pass away" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "will disappear" | "Disappear" for modern idiom. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "will pass away" — the traditional rendering is both accurate and memorable. "Disappear" loses the processual quality of παρέρχομαι.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 13:32 — "nor the Son" — all three include; formatting differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "neither the angels in heaven nor the Son — except the Father" | Em-dash emphasis. |
| LOGOS | "not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father" | Commas; "but only." |
| PNEUMA | "Not the angels in heaven. Not even the Son. Only the Father." | Short sentences for emphasis. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father" — LOGOS's punctuation balances emphasis and readability. PNEUMA's fragmented sentences are dramatic but depart from the single-clause Greek construction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:35 — "when the rooster crows" vs. "cock-crowing" vs. "dawn" for ἀλεκτοροφωνίας

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "at-cock-crowing" | Literal; preserves the night-watch term. |
| LOGOS | "when the rooster crows" | Explains the watch designation. |
| PNEUMA | "dawn" | Interprets; "dawn" replaces the rooster reference. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "when the rooster crows" — preserves the watch designation and its deliberate connection to Peter's denial (14:30, 72). PNEUMA's "dawn" severs this crucial literary link.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The rooster/Peter connection is essential.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Apocalyptic vocabulary** (abomination, tribulation, false christs, birth pains) | vv. 8, 14, 19, 22 | Traditional terms retained for intertextual and theological resonance |
| **PNEUMA's modernizing** (cosmic, courts, contractions, disgusting sacrilege) | vv. 9, 14, 25 | Generally not adopted; biblical register maintained |
| **Literary connections** (rooster crows, "let the reader understand") | vv. 14, 35 | Cross-references preserved |
| **Sentence structure** (v. 32 fragmentation) | v. 32 | Greek clause structure respected |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 13 is the most densely apocalyptic chapter in Mark, and the disagreements cluster around apocalyptic vocabulary. PNEUMA consistently attempts to make opaque apocalyptic language accessible ("disgusting sacrilege," "fake messiahs," "cosmic powers"), but the consensus retains the traditional terms because they function as signals within a larger biblical framework. The most consequential decision is preserving "when the rooster crows" in v. 35, which maintains Mark's deliberate foreshadowing of Peter's denial in chapter 14. The chapter shows that apocalyptic discourse demands a higher register than narrative prose, and the consensus appropriately maintains that register.
